In Situ Polymerization: As mentioned earlier, in situ polymerization involves dispersing clay particles in the monomer before polymerization. This approach is particularly effective at improving nanoparticle dispersion and achieving exfoliation, leading to significant improvements in nanocomposite properties. Ultrasonication: Ultrasonication can be used in combination with solution mixing or direct mixing to enhance the dispersion of clay nanoparticles. The high-energy ultrasonic waves break apart agglomerates, improving the exfoliation of the clay layers and ensuring better distribution in the epoxy matrix. The design and synthesis of clay nanoparticle-reinforced epoxy resin nanocomposites have shown significant potential for improving the performance of epoxy-based materials. By leveraging surface modification techniques and optimizing the dispersion of clay particles, researchers have been able to enhance the mechanical, thermal, and barrier properties of these nanocomposites. In a study carried out by Wang et al. 2020, the clay-reinforced epoxy nanocomposite was prepared by the polymerization method. The effect of clay addition on the mechanical properties of epoxyclay nanocomposites was studied through tensile, flexural, impact strength, and fracture toughness tests. The morphology and tribology behavior of epoxyclay nanocomposites were determined by X-ray diffraction XRD and wear test, respectively. The wear test was performed to determine the specific abrasion of the nanocomposites. In addition, the water absorption characteristic of the nanocomposites was also investigated in this study. XRD analysis indicated that the exfoliation structure was observed in the epoxy nanocomposites with 3 wt of clay, while the intercalated structure was shown at 6 wt of clay. It was found that the addition of clay up to 3 wt increased the tensile strength, flexural strength, impact strength, and the fracture toughness. On the contrary, the presence of above 3 wt of clay produced a reverse effect. It could be concluded that the best properties in mechanical, wear resistance, and water resistance were obtained for the epoxy nanocomposites containing 3 wt of clay. Zhang et al. 2019 carried out an experiment to examine the influence of three 3 different processing techniques on the properties of epoxy-montmorillonite Clay nanocomposites. Epoxy resin was mixed with varying concentrations of montmorillonite clay up to 5 wt. The three processing techniques compared were mechanical mixing, ultrasonication, and in situ polymerization as listed above. The curing agent used was a standard amine- based system. Characterization included tensile testing, thermal gravimetric analysis TGA, and scanning electron microscopy SEM. The results obtained showed that the method of dispersion significantly impacted the composites properties. Ultrasonication and in situ polymerization produced better clay exfoliation and dispersion, resulting in superior tensile properties compared to mechanical mixing, SEM images showed more uniform nanoparticle distribution when ultrasonication was used and thermal stability, measured by TGA, was enhanced in nanocomposites prepared using ultrasonication. APPLICATION OF EPOXY REINFORCED COATING ON MILD STEEL Numerous experimental studies have demonstrated the efficacy of clay-reinforced epoxy coatings in improving the corrosion resistance of mild steel. A study by Sari et al. 2018 investigated the use of organo-montmorillonite OMMT nanoparticles in epoxy coatings applied to steel. The results showed a significant reduction in water permeability and corrosion rate, with electrochemical impedance spectroscopy EIS confirming the superior barrier properties of the nanocomposite coating compared to pure epoxy coatings. Similarly, Wang et al. 2020 conducted research on the use of MMT-reinforced epoxy coatings for protecting steel in marine environments. The study found that exfoliated MMT layers created an effective barrier against chloride ion penetration, which is a critical factor in pitting corrosion. The MMT-modified epoxy coatings exhibited lower corrosion current density and higher impedance values in salt spray tests, confirming their effectiveness in harsh environments. Another study by Kumar and Bajaj 2019 explored the synergistic effect of clay nanoparticles and graphene oxide GO in epoxy coatings applied to carbon steel. The hybrid nanocomposite coating exhibited enhanced mechanical and anticorrosion properties due to the combined effects of the layered silicate structure of clay and the impermeable nature of GO. This hybrid approach further improved the durability of the coating and its resistance to aggressive chemical environments. REVIEW OF RELATED WORK DONE ON EPOXY REINFORCEMENT Epoxy resins are extensively used in a wide range of industrial applications, consisting of coatings, adhesives, composites, and electronics. However, similar to their reinforcement with clay nanoparticles, epoxy resins are often brittle, and their mechanical, thermal, and electrical properties can be limiting factors. To address these issues, researchers have investigated various reinforcement strategies by incorporating different materials such as carbon- based nanomaterials, glass and carbon fibers, natural fibers, and metallic nanoparticles into epoxy matrices. A study by L. Monti et al. 2019 researched the reinforcement of epoxy coatings with graphene oxide GO, aiming to exploit the materials impermeability and mechanical properties to improve corrosion resistance. The addition of 1 wt. GO resulted in a 65 reduction in water vapor permeability. The composite exhibited enhanced mechanical strength and a significant increase in corrosion resistance as shown by electrochemical testing. Graphene oxide is highly effective at improving both the mechanical and corrosion resistance of epoxy coatings, making it a promising material for protective coatings. Due to graphenes high surface area, it is difficult to prevent agglomeration during mixing with the epoxy matrix. This agglomeration reduces the effectiveness of graphene in creating a uniform barrier to corrosive agents. Graphene oxide is still relatively expensive to produce in large quantities, limiting its application in industries that require cost-effective solutions. Graphene oxide significantly improves the performance of epoxy coatings, but cost and dispersion remain major hurdles to its widespread industrial use. A study by S. Moradi, et al. 2018 explored the use of multi-walled carbon nanotubes MWCNTs as reinforcements in epoxy coatings to enhance mechanical properties and corrosion resistance. The optimal loading of 1.5 wt. MWCNTs improved mechanical strength and barrier properties, significantly lowering the corrosion rate. Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy EIS showed higher impedance values for the MWCNT-reinforced epoxy, indicating improved corrosion resistance. MWCNTs contribute to the formation of a more impermeable epoxy matrix, effectively reducing the penetration of corrosive agents. However, achieving uniform dispersion and alignment of MWCNTs in the epoxy matrix is challenging. Poor dispersion can lead to localized weak points in the coating, reducing both mechanical strength and corrosion resistance. Health and Safety Concerns: MWCNTs pose health risks if inhaled during the production process, leading to concerns about safe handling and manufacturing processes. MWCNTs enhance the barrier properties of epoxy coatings, but ensuring proper dispersion and addressing health concerns are essential for their practical application. A study by A. Bahrami, et al. 2021 examined the incorporation of zinc oxide ZnO nanoparticles into epoxy coatings to improve their barrier and self-healing properties. ZnO nanoparticles 2 wt. improved corrosion resistance in salt spray tests and enhanced the self-healing function by releasing protective zinc ions. The coating also exhibited increased UV resistance and mechanical durability, making it suitable for outdoor environments. While ZnO nanoparticles can offer self-healing properties, the effectiveness of this self-healing is limited to small cracks and damages. Larger-scale mechanical damage may not be effectively repaired by the zinc ions alone. Similar to other nanoparticles, ZnO can suffer from poor dispersion in the epoxy matrix, which can negatively affect the coatings performance. ZnO nanoparticles enhance corrosion resistance and add self-healing functionality, but their effectiveness is constrained by agglomeration and limited healing capabilities for larger-scale damage. Y. Huang, et al. 2020 investigated the use of polyaniline PANI as a conductive polymer reinforcement for epoxy coatings, leveraging its electrochemical properties for corrosion protection. Epoxy-PANI coatings significantly improved corrosion resistance, particularly in chloride-rich environments. Electrochemical testing confirmed the formation of a passivation layer on the metal substrate, reducing corrosion rates. However, achieving uniform dispersion of PANI in the epoxy matrix is difficult. Additionally, PANIs compatibility with the epoxy resin can sometimes be suboptimal, leading to phase separation. PANIs corrosion-inhibiting properties can be influenced by environmental factors such as humidity, pH, and temperature, which may limit its effectiveness in certain conditions. Polyaniline provides excellent electrochemical corrosion protection, but ensuring its stable dispersion and maintaining its performance across varying environmental conditions remain key challenges. M. Montazerian, et al. 2020 developed a hybrid epoxy coating reinforced with both silica nanoparticles and polyaniline PANI to combine the barrier properties of silica with the electrochemical passivation provided by PANI. The hybrid coating exhibited better corrosion resistance than single-component coatings, reducing corrosion rates by over 70. The combination of silica and PANI provided both physical and active electrochemical protection. However, achieving an optimal balance between silica and PANI in the epoxy matrix is complex and can affect the overall performance of the coating. Too much silica can hinder the conductive properties of PANI, while too much PANI can reduce the mechanical strength of the coating. The combination of multiple reinforcement materials increases the cost of the formulation, which could be a limiting factor for large-scale applications. Hybrid epoxy coatings offer superior corrosion resistance by combining the strengths of different reinforcement materials, but the formulation complexity and cost are challenges for widespread use. RESEARCH GAPS Despite significant advancements in corrosion science, a potential gap is lack of environmentally friendly and cost- effective corrosion protection methods. Traditional corrosion inhibitors, coatings, and protective treatments often involve toxic compounds, heavy metals like chromates and lead-based additives, and complex manufacturing processes that pose environmental hazards and increase operational costs. Environmental Concerns with Conventional Methods Widely used corrosion protection systems such as chromate-based inhibitors and solvent-based paints are known for their high efficiency. However, they are also associated with toxicity, poor biodegradability, and long-term ecological risks Wang et al., 2016; Li et al., 2018. These substances can leach into soil and water, affecting aquatic ecosystems and human health. The Push for Green Alternatives Recent research has shifted toward "green corrosion inhibitors", primarily plant extracts, biodegradable polymers, and natural substances. These eco-friendly options have shown potential, but many lack long-term durability, temperature resistance, and compatibility with industrial environments Umoren et al., 2019; Rani and Basu, 2015. For instance, inhibitors derived from neem, green tea, and garlic extracts have demonstrated success in laboratory settings but often degrade rapidly under operational conditions, limiting their real-world application Quraishi et al., 2017. Cost Barriers and Scalability Issues Another gap is the cost-effectiveness and scalability of environmentally friendly alternatives. Biopolymer-based coatings, sol-gel techniques, and nanotechnology-enhanced systems offer promise, but require expensive raw materials, equipment, or energy-intensive synthesis processes Liu et al., 2021; Al-Turaif, 2019. This reduces their appeal for widespread industrial use, especially in low income regions. Need for Hybrid and Multifunctional Solutions An emerging direction is the development of hybrid coatings and nanocomposites that combine corrosion resistance with environmental safety and cost efficiency. For example, clay-reinforced epoxy nanocomposites and zinc phosphatechitosan hybrids offer low toxicity alternatives, but optimization for various industrial conditions is still ongoing Zhang et al., 2020; Sangeetha et al., 2022. RESEARCH GAPS ADDRESSED BY THIS STUDY This study strategically targets these gaps by formulating and testing nanocomposite coatings that incorporate clay nanoparticles into epoxy matrices, with future prospects for manganese oxide-enhanced systems. The combined use of these materials offers a path toward sustainable, high-performance anti-corrosion coatings. Clay Nanoparticles as Functional Reinforcements - Clay nanoparticles provide a dual function in coating systems: They act as mechanical reinforcements, increasing the hardness and toughness of the epoxy matrix. Structurally, they introduce a tortuous path that slows down the ingress of corrosive species e.g., water, oxygen, chloride ions, thereby enhancing barrier performance Zhao et al., 2019; Muthukumar and Ramya, 2021. Their natural abundance, low cost, and nontoxicity make clay a viable candidate for green coating technologies. Epoxy as a Versatile Base Matrix - Epoxy remains one of the most reliable materials in anti-corrosion applications due to its: Strong adhesion to metallic substrates Low permeability Chemical inertness When modified with additives like clay or manganese oxide, its protective properties can be significantly improved, enhancing both corrosion resistance and durability under aggressive environmental conditions Li et al., 2020; Karim et al., 2022.

The approach adopted in this study offers a meaningful contribution to closing a vital technological and environmental gap in corrosion protection. By leveraging clays natural properties, enhancing it within an epoxy matrix, and exploring future hybridization with manganese oxide, this research promotes a next generation, eco- conscious protective system that is affordable, effective, and industrially scalable. CHAPTER THREE METHODOLOGY This chapter outlines the materials required for the investigation as well as the particular steps in the method for evaluating the effectiveness of clay nanoparticles embedded in epoxy resin as an organic anti-corrosion coating for mild steel. The goal was to enhance corrosion resistance by employing nanomaterials as reinforcement agents. MATERIALS The following resources were used in the research and are shown in table 3.1 below: Table 3.1: Materials Selected and their description SUBSTRATE PREPARATION Mild steel samples used in this study were sourced from a cylindrical crude oil pipeline and were prepared as square coupons measuring 5 cm  5 cm  0.6 cm. To examine the surface features and composition of the metal prior to coating, Scanning Electron Microscopy SEM and Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy EDX analyses were performed. These analyses provided detailed insights into the material's microstructure and elemental composition, as shown in Table 3.2. Each steel sample underwent a multistep polishing process using silicon carbide abrasive papers with grit sizes of 60, 80, 250, 400 and 600. After polishing, the specimens were rinsed with distilled water and degreased using acetone to remove any contaminants. A 5 mm hole was drilled at the top-center edge of each sample to allow for easy suspension during coating and drying. Strings were threaded through the holes and labeled with numbered tags for proper identification during testing and handling. This preparation ensured consistency across all test samples and allowed for smooth, repeatable application and evaluation of the protective coatings. Table 3.2: Elemental Composition of Mild Steel Sample Source: Experimental data 2024 EXPERIMENT DESIGN To ensure a systematic and statistically sound investigation, the experimental framework was built using Response Surface Methodology RSM, a widely used optimization technique in materials science. Specifically, the Central Composite Design CCD approach, available in MINITAB-17 software, was used to develop the design matrix. The study examined the influence of three key variables on corrosion resistance: Hydrochloric acid concentration moldm³, Weight percentage of clay nanoparticles  wt, Exposure duration days. A total of twenty 20 experimental runs were generated by the software to explore the interactions between these factors and their combined effects on corrosion behavior. Each experimental combination was applied to a coated mild steel sample for testing. This design methodology not only reduced the number of trials needed but also allowed for the identification of optimal conditions through regression and surface response modeling, ensuring robust data interpretation and statistical significance Jin et al. 2019, Kumar et al. 2021. Table 3.3: Design Matrix for Coating Formulation and Testing This structured approach to experimentation provided a rich dataset for analyzing not only the direct effects of each factor but also their interactions, paving the way for the optimization phase of the study. CLAY NANOPARTICLE SYNTHESIS The process of synthesizing clay nanoparticles began with the collection of natural clay from a local source. This raw clay was manually sorted to remove visible impurities such as sand, stones, organic debris, and plant matter. The purified clay was then thoroughly dried to eliminate moisture, first by sun-drying over a period of three days, and then by heating in a microwave laboratory oven at 150C for two hours to ensure a more uniform dryness. Once fully dried, the clay was ground using a mechanical grinder, applying consistent shearing force to reduce the particle size. The initial coarse grinding was followed by repeated cycles of grinding and sieving using a Manikarn test sieve to progressively refine the particle size toward the nanoscale. An industrial magnetic sieve shaker was employed to classify particles according to size, and oversized grains were returned for further milling. This extended processing lasting up to 78 hours allowed the clay to reach an average particle size close to 100 microns. Although not in the sub-100 nm range of typical nanoparticles, this size was sufficient to improve dispersion within epoxy matrices and significantly influence the composites barrier properties. The resulting clay powder was sealed in an airtight container to prevent contamination or moisture absorption before being used in coating preparation. Studies such as those by Alhassan et al. 2017 and Muthukumar  Ramya 2021 have shown that well processed clay particles enhance the mechanical and corrosion-resistant properties of polymer coatings by forming a tortuous path for corrosive agents and reinforcing the epoxy matrix. a Unprocessed raw clay b Ground and processed clay powder Fig. 3.0: Clay Samples COATING PREPARATION, APPLICATION, AND CURING Following the experimental design created through Response Surface Methodology RSM, twenty distinct epoxy- clay nanocomposite coatings were prepared. Each formulation varied in the concentration of clay nanoparticles, ranging from 1 wt. to 5 wt., according to the matrix outlined in Table 3.3. Using a digital electronic balance, precise quantities of epoxy resin, hardener, and clay nanoparticles were measured. These components were handled with care to ensure consistency in composition across all samples. The preparation process began by mixing the epoxy resin and hardener in a 2:1 ratio, following the manufacturers instructions. This step ensured a strong chemical cross-linking of the base matrix. The mixture was stirred thoroughly until a homogeneous solution was achieved. Next, the pre-weighed clay nanoparticles were slowly incorporated into the resin-hardener blend. The addition was done gradually and with constant stirring to prevent agglomeration and to ensure even dispersion throughout the matrix. The entire mixture was stirred for an additional 30 minutes using an overhead electric stirrer at a controlled speed of 610 rpm, a method supported by studies like Jin et al. 2019 and Karim et al. 2022 for optimal nanoparticle distribution. Once mixing was complete, each mild steel coupon a total of 20 carbon steel specimens was dipped into the corresponding coating formulation. The dipping method was selected to ensure uniform film thickness and comprehensive surface coverage. After immersion, excess coating was allowed to drip off before the specimens were suspended vertically for drying. Curing was carried out by air-drying the coated samples under direct sunlight for 72 hours, a process sufficient for full polymerization and hardening of the epoxy. This curing method not only saved energy but also demonstrated the feasibility of using low-cost, eco-friendly processing techniques. a Mixing process of epoxy and clay nanoparticles b Dipping of mild steel coupons in prepared coating Fig. 3.1: Mixing process of coating and Dipping of mild steel coupons in coating This meticulous preparation and curing procedure laid the foundation for reliable performance evaluation during the corrosion testing phase. ELECTROCHEMICAL CORROSION MEASUREMENT To assess the corrosion resistance of the coated steel samples, a series of electrochemical tests were conducted using standardized protocols. These tests provided insights into how effectively each coating formulation protected the metal surface in a corrosive environment. EXPERIMENTAL SETUP The corrosion tests were performed using a Model 600E Series Electrochemical Workstation developed by CH Instruments, as shown in Figure 3.3. This setup included a three-electrode cell configuration connected to a computer running CHI software version 12.04 for data acquisition and OriginLab 2024 for advanced plotting and analysis. The setup involved: Working Electrode WE: The coated steel specimen with a surface area of 16 cm² exposed to the electrolyte. Reference Electrode RE: A stable AgAgCl electrode. Counter Electrode CE: A platinum mesh that completed the circuit. The three-electrode system allowed precise control of the samples electrical potential while monitoring the corresponding electrochemical response which is key for evaluating corrosion behavior Zhao et al., 2019; Aljourani et al., 2020. Fig. 3.2: Model 600E Electrochemical Analyzer OPEN CIRCUIT POTENTIAL OCP Before any polarization was applied, the Open Circuit Potential OCP of each sample was measured. This step determined the natural potential difference between the working and reference electrodes without any current flow. OCP readings were recorded after 300 seconds of immersion in the electrolyte to allow the system to stabilize. This baseline potential offered initial insights into the thermodynamic tendency of the coated metal to corrode. POTENTIODYNAMIC POLARIZATION This method was used to investigate the electrochemical performance of each coated steel sample under aggressive chemical exposure. After the OCP was stabilized, a voltage sweep was applied in the range of 1.5 V to 1.5 V at a scan rate of 0.01 Vs. The scan started at the stabilized OCP and recorded current responses across both the anodic corrosion-promoting and cathodic corrosion-inhibiting directions. The resulting data were used to calculate: Corrosion potential Ecorr Corrosion current density icorr Polarization resistance Rp Tafel slopes for both anodic βa and cathodic βc reactions These values were derived using Tafel extrapolation techniques, allowing a deeper understanding of the corrosion kinetics and the protective quality of each coating formulation Panahi et al., 2024; Muthukumar  Ramya, 2021. ELECTROCHEMICAL IMPEDANCE SPECTROSCOPY EIS For a more comprehensive evaluation, Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy EIS was also performed. This technique measures how the coating resists the flow of alternating current AC at various frequencies. EIS was initiated at the OCP using a small AC signal with: Amplitude: 0.005 V Frequency range: 100 kHz High Freq to 1 Hz Low Freq Impedance data including real Z' and imaginary Z" components were collected and plotted on Nyquist and Bode graphs. These were then fitted to an equivalent circuit model Figure 3.5 to extract parameters such as: Solution resistance Rs Charge transfer resistance Rct Double-layer capacitance Cdl Cole-Cole resistance CC These values are critical for understanding the barrier properties and ionic resistance of the coatings Karim et al., 2022; Joseph et al., 2022. Fig. 3.3: EIS Testing Setup Fig. 3.4: Equivalent Electrical Circuit Model for Data Fitting MODELING AND STATISTICAL ANALYSIS OF CORROSION RATE After collecting corrosion rate data from electrochemical tests, the next step was to analyze and model this information to understand the interactions between the variables and identify optimal performance conditions. This was done using Minitab-17, a statistical software well suited for Design of Experiments DOE and Response Surface Methodology RSM. Approach and Tools Used Key statistical tools and techniques applied included: Main effects plots  to visualize the individual influence of each variable. Contour and surface plots  to assess interaction effects between two variables at a time.

These were then fitted to an equivalent circuit model Figure 3.5 to extract parameters such as: Solution resistance Rs Charge transfer resistance Rct Double-layer capacitance Cdl Cole-Cole resistance CC These values are critical for understanding the barrier properties and ionic resistance of the coatings Karim et al., 2022; Joseph et al., 2022. Fig. 3.3: EIS Testing Setup Fig. 3.4: Equivalent Electrical Circuit Model for Data Fitting MODELING AND STATISTICAL ANALYSIS OF CORROSION RATE After collecting corrosion rate data from electrochemical tests, the next step was to analyze and model this information to understand the interactions between the variables and identify optimal performance conditions. This was done using Minitab-17, a statistical software well suited for Design of Experiments DOE and Response Surface Methodology RSM. Approach and Tools Used Key statistical tools and techniques applied included: Main effects plots  to visualize the individual influence of each variable. Contour and surface plots  to assess interaction effects between two variables at a time. Analysis of Variance ANOVA  to test the statistical significance of linear, quadratic, and interaction terms. The model was constructed to predict the corrosion rate based on: Clay nanoparticle concentration  weight, Acid concentration moldm³, Exposure time days. A significance level of p  0.05 was used to determine which model terms were meaningful, ensuring reliability of the regression results Farag et al., 2021. Model Performance Evaluation To assess how well the model fitted the data: The R-squared R² value was compared to the Predicted R², to evaluate how accurately the model could forecast new results. The Adjusted R² accounted for the number of predictors used, helping to avoid overfitting. Only models with high and closely matching R² and predicted R² values were accepted as robust. Validation To confirm the models accuracy, predicted corrosion rates were compared to the actual experimental values. The closeness of these values validated the mathematical relationships derived from the statistical modeling. This approach not only helped identify optimal process conditions but also ensured that the findings could be reliably repeated in practical scenarios. Such modeling techniques are increasingly used in corrosion research and nanocomposite coatings development Rajendran et al., 2020; Singh et al., 2022. OPTIMIZATION APPROACH Once the statistical models were validated, the next objective was to determine the best combination of variables that would result in minimal corrosion rates. This optimization process was carried out using the Desirability Function Approach DFA within the Response Surface Methodology RSM framework in Minitab. Desirability Function Overview The desirability function converts multiple response variables in this case, corrosion rate into a single metric, d, which ranges from 0 undesirable to 1 highly desirable. A higher desirability value indicates a better performing combination of factors. The aim was to: Minimize the corrosion rate, Maintain optimal weight  of clay, Ensure reasonable acid concentration and exposure duration. The optimization algorithm searched for the most favorable blend of input parameters that led to the lowest predicted corrosion rate without compromising on other coating characteristics like adhesion and hardness. This multi-variable optimization technique has been effectively used in similar materials and surface protection studies Chatterjee et al., 2018; Daramola et al., 2021, especially where economic and performance trade-offs must be balanced. The outcomes of this stage informed the final coating formulation, which was then subjected to validation and further testing in the subsequent phase of the research. VALIDATION AND PROTECTIVE EFFICIENCY OF THE OPTIMAL COATINGS After identifying the most promising coating formulation through statistical optimization, the next step was to validate its actual performance in real-world conditions. This was done by conducting additional electrochemical corrosion tests on new samples coated with the optimal blend of epoxy and clay nanoparticles. These samples were prepared and tested using the same procedures described earlier for: Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy EIS Potentiodynamic Polarization PDP Purpose of Validation The goal of this stage was to: Compare the experimentally observed corrosion rate of the optimal coating to the predicted values from the RSM model. Assess the consistency and predictive accuracy of the statistical model under practical conditions. The validation confirmed that the optimized coating provided significantly higher corrosion resistance, in alignment with the predicted values, demonstrating the models reliability and effectiveness. This approach ensures that the study's recommendations are not only theoretical but also experimentally confirmed, a best practice also adopted in modern corrosion protection research Adewole et al., 2019; Hassan et al., 2022. The successful validation strengthened the confidence in using this formulation for industrial-scale applications, where consistent corrosion protection is critical. SURFACE CHARACTERIZATION OF THE SAMPLE AFTER CORROSION To gain deeper insight into how each coating performed under corrosive attack, the surface morphology of the mild steel samples was analyzed after exposure to the corrosive environment. This was done using a Scanning Electron Microscope SEM, model JOEL-JSM 7600F, which provides high resolution imaging of the surface. These post- corrosion images helped identify the type of corrosion that occurred e.g., pitting, uniform, intergranular, the integrity and breakdown of the coating, and the extent of surface damage on both coated and uncoated specimens. Why Surface Characterization Matters This microscopic evaluation was crucial for validating the visual and electrochemical data, understanding the mechanism of protection offered by the clay-reinforced epoxy coatings, and revealing the barrier effect and coating failures, if any. Uncoated or poorly protected samples often displayed corrosion pits, surface roughness, and oxide formation, whereas the optimized coatings showed smooth, uniform and minimally damaged surfaces which is a sign of effective corrosion resistance. This stage offered qualitative confirmation of the results obtained from electrochemical measurements and statistical analysis, which is a standard practice in modern material testing Kavitha et al., 2021; Joseph  Uday, 2023. COMPARISON OF CORROSION INHIBITION PROPERTIES WITH CONVENTIONAL COATINGS To evaluate the practical viability of the newly developed epoxyclay nanocomposite coating, its performance was compared against two conventional commercial coatings commonly used for mild steel protection: Paint A HINAZ Paint B SIKAFLEX Paint O  the optimized epoxy-clay formulation from this study The Adhesion test and Hardness test were conducted to assess and compare their mechanical and adhesive properties, which play a critical role in real-world corrosion protection. ADHESION TEST The cross-cut tape adhesion test was conducted following the guidelines of ASTM D3359. Using a sharp knife and a steel cutting guide, each coated specimen was scored with a grid pattern of cuts, with uniform spacing as specified by the dry film thickness. The cuts penetrated the coating to the metal substrate. Next, a pressure-sensitive adhesive tape was firmly applied over the grid and then peeled off at a controlled angle and speed. The degree to which the coating detached from the substrate was used to assess adhesion quality. Each specimen was rated on a scale from 0B to 5B, where: 5B indicates perfect adhesion with no detachment, 0B indicates complete failure of adhesion. Paint O the optimized epoxy-clay coating consistently scored higher than the commercial coatings, displaying minimal to no peeling, a clear indication of superior bonding to the steel substrate. Visual aids from Figure 3.5 and Figure 3.6 provided a grading reference for consistent evaluation. This test highlighted the improved interfacial strength and film integrity provided by the nanoparticle-reinforced system, a trend also noted in studies by Kaur et al. 2018 and Chakraborty et al. 2020. Fig. 3.5: The cross-cut adhesion of samples. Fig. 3.6 Grade classification table of adhesion test Source: ASTM D3359, 2010 HARDNESS TEST Mechanical robustness of the coatings was further assessed using a Rockwell Hardness Tester Model HR-150A with a C-scale diamond cone indenter. Heres how the test was performed: A preload of 10 kg was applied to ensure stable contact. Then a major load of 150 N was maintained for 15 seconds. The resulting indentation depth was measured to calculate the Rockwell hardness value HRC using the formula: HRC  100  0.002  h, where h is the depth of the indentation in microns. Each coating was tested 20 times to ensure consistency, and the average hardness values were calculated. The optimized coating Paint O demonstrated notably higher hardness values compared to Paints A and B, reflecting enhanced resistance to mechanical wear and surface deformation. This improvement is attributed to the nanoclays reinforcing effect, which creates a more rigid and durable matrix Rajeswari et al., 2021. Fig. 3.7 illustrates the Rockwell Hardness HR-150A testing setup. CHAPTER FOUR RESULTS AND DISCUSSION This chapter presents and interprets the experimental findings gathered through surface morphology analysis, electrochemical testing, statistical modeling, and post-experiment material evaluation. The results are discussed in the context of corrosion behavior, coating performance and how the addition of clay nanoparticles influenced protective efficiency. SURFACE MORPHOLOGY AND COMPOSITION OF MILD STEEL BEFORE COATING The chemical makeup and surface structure of the uncoated mild steel were first evaluated using Scanning Electron Microscopy SEM and Energy-Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy EDX. These tests revealed a relatively uniform but slightly rough surface, which is typical for industrial-grade carbon steel. Fig. 4.0: The SEM image of the mild steel Fig. 4.1: EDX spectra of the mild steel Table 4.1: Elemental Composition of the Mild steel The EDX analysis confirmed that iron Fe made up the bulk of the alloy, along with smaller quantities of carbon C, manganese Mn, silicon Si, copper Cu, and aluminum Al, all of which contribute to the mechanical strength and corrosion behavior of the material. Understanding this baseline composition was essential in predicting how well the coatings would bond to the surface and how the steel might behave in a corrosive environment Zhu et al., 2018; Ahmad et al., 2020. SEM ANALYSIS OF SYNTHESIZED CLAY NANOPARTICLES The surface morphology and structural features of the synthesized clay nanoparticles were examined using Scanning Electron Microscopy SEM at two magnification levels to provide a detailed understanding of their physical characteristics and potential functional advantages in coating applications. Fig. 4.2 A: 10,000x magnification Fig. 4.2 B: 8,000x magnification Figure 4.2 A presents the clay nanoparticles at 10,000 magnification with a scale bar of 50 µm. At this high magnification, the particles display a markedly porous and irregular surface texture, with numerous voids and microcavities visible across the structure. Such surface porosity increases the available surface area, which is beneficial for enhancing interfacial adhesion in coating systems and can contribute to improved barrier properties against corrosive agents. Figure 4.2 B shows the nanoparticles at 8,000 magnification with a scale bar of 20 µm, offering a broader yet still detailed view of particle distribution and packing. At this magnification, the pore openings appear more pronounced, and the particle boundaries are more clearly distinguishable. The irregular arrangements, coupled with the presence of void spaces, suggest that the synthesized clay possesses high surface heterogeneity, a property that can facilitate strong mechanical interlocking within epoxy matrices. The combination of observations from both magnifications suggests that the clay nanoparticles are well-suited for reinforcing polymer coatings due to their large surface area, rough surface topology, and potential for strong interfacial bonding. These morphological characteristics are critical for improving coating performance in corrosion protection applications, particularly in aggressive environments where adhesion and impermeability are vital. ELECTROCHEMICAL IMPEDANCE SPECTROSCOPY EIS RESULTS AND ANALYSIS Table 4.2: The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy test result of the 20 coated sample of mild steel Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy EIS was employed to evaluate the corrosion resistance performance of clay-reinforced epoxy coatings applied to mild steel under varying concentrations of hydrochloric acid HCl. A total of 20 coated samples were assessed, incorporating clay nanoparticle CNP loadings of 1 wt., 3 wt., and 5 wt., alongside control samples containing 0 wt. clay. The acid concentrations tested were 0.5 M, 1.5 M, and 2.5 M, thereby simulating different levels of corrosive severity. Table 4.2 presents key EIS parameters obtained from the tests which include; Charge Transfer Resistance Rp - a direct measure of the coatings resistance to corrosion; higher Rp values indicate superior protection. Solution Resistance Ru - representing the ionic resistance of the HCl medium. Capacitance Y₀ - indicative of coating porosity and moisture uptake capacity. Dispersion Coefficient α - a measure of surface heterogeneity and roughness. Key trends observed are; Samples with higher clay content generally exhibited greater Rp values, confirming the role of CNPs in enhancing barrier performance. Control samples 0 wt. clay recorded the lowest Rp values, demonstrating weaker resistance and higher vulnerability to acid attack. Variations in Ru, Y₀, and α further reflected differences in coating density and uniformity across the formulations. The Nyquist and Bode plots Figures 4.3, 4.4 and 4.5 illustrate these findings. The Nyquist plots exhibited well- defined semicircular arcs, characteristic of charge-transfer-dominated corrosion mechanisms. Sample 14 5 wt. CNPs in 0.5 M HCl displayed one of the largest arc diameters Rp  532 Ω, indicating excellent corrosion resistance, whereas Sample 20 0 wt. CNPs in 2.5 M HCl showed the smallest arc Rp  210 Ω, signifying poor performance. Fig. 4.3: Nyquist plot of samples 10,9,8,7,12  11. Bode plot of samples 2,4  6. Fig. 4.4: Nyquist plot of samples 14,12,10  8. Bode plot of samples 16  8. Figure 4.5: Bode plot of samples 8,10  12. The Bode plots further reinforced these results with higher impedance magnitudes observed for CNP-reinforced coatings and a sharp impedance drop in non-reinforced controls. Increasing clay nanoparticle content significantly reduced ionic conductivity through the coating, particularly under low-acid conditions, whereas higher acid concentrations diminished protection by accelerating coating degradation. These impedance behaviors align with the corrosion rate data and validate the contribution of clay nanoparticles toward improving the electrochemical stability of epoxy coatings in acidic environments. SUMMARY OF CHARGE TRANSFER RESISTANCE MODEL A statistical regression model was developed to describe the influence of clay nanoparticle content CNPs and hydrochloric acid concentration HCl on the measured charge transfer resistance Rp. The model equation, expressed in uncoded units is: Rp Ω  420.9  106.6 CNPs  112.4 HCl  16.28  2CNPs  17.8  2HCl  12.63 CNPs  HCl This equation reveals several important trends: Positive coefficient for CNPs 106.6 indicates a strong positive effect of increasing clay content on Rp. Negative coefficient for HCl 112.4 confirms that higher acid concentrations significantly reduce corrosion resistance. Non-linear quadratic effects - the CNPs² term has a notable negative coefficient, suggesting diminishing returns at higher loadings, while the HCl² term shows a smaller, positive but less significant effect. Interaction term CNPs  HCl - a positive value 12.63 indicating that the impact of clay nanoparticles depends partly on the acid concentration. Table 4.3: Summary of ANOVA results The table summarizes the ANOVA results, which confirm that the model is statistically significant P  0.05 for most terms. The  wt. clay variable had the strongest effect F  93.53, P  0.000, followed by acid concentration P  0.012 which is greater than 0.005 but still less than 0.05, suggesting it has a statistically significant but relatively weaker effect compared to  wt clay. The quadratic effect of CNPs was also significant, while the quadratic effect of acid concentration was not, having a high P-value of 0.294. . Table 4.4: Model summary of charge transfer resistance The table shows the models goodness-of-fit: R²  90.74 - indicating that the model explains over 90 of the variation in Rp. Adjusted R²  87.44 - accounting for the number of predictors. Predicted R²  78.95 - reflecting strong predictive capability, albeit slightly lower than the adjusted R², suggesting mild overfitting. Standard deviation S  33.4249 - showing the average deviation of observed values from model predictions. Overall, the regression model effectively captures the relationship between clay content, acid concentration, and corrosion resistance. The statistical significance of the interaction term also highlights that the protective benefits of clay nanoparticles are more pronounced in less aggressive acidic environments, while their impact diminishes as acid concentration rises. MAIN EFFECT AND INTERACTION PLOTS The main effect plots provide a visual representation of how each individual factor - clay nanoparticle content CNPs and acid concentration HCl - influences polarization resistance Rp. Figure 4.6 shows the main effect of CNPs on Rp. Fig. 4.6: Main effect plot of polarization resistance and  weight of CNPs As the clay content increases from 0 to approximately 3.84.0 wt., the polarization resistance rises steadily, indicating improved barrier protection and reduced corrosion susceptibility. Beyond this range, Rp begins to decline slightly, suggesting that excessive clay loading may lead to particle agglomeration or structural defects in the coating, which could reduce its protective efficiency. Figure 4.7 depicts the main effect of acid concentration on Rp. Fig. 4.7: main effect plot of polarization resistance and acid concentration. There is a consistent inverse relationship between HCl concentration and polarization resistance - higher acid concentrations accelerate the degradation of the coating, resulting in lower Rp values. This is consistent with the known aggressive nature of acidic environments, which can break down epoxy matrices and weaken interfacial adhesion. Figures 4.8 and 4.9 illustrate the interaction effects between CNP content and HCl concentration using contour and 3D surface plots, respectively. In the contour plot Figure 4.8, darker green regions correspond to the highest Rp values  550 Ω, which occur when clay content is high and acid concentration is low. Conversely, black areas 300 Ω represent the lowest resistance, typically at low CNP loadings combined with high acid concentration. The 3D surface plot Figure 4.9 visually confirms this interaction, showing that polarization resistance is maximized at high clay loadings and low acid concentrations, while steep drops in Rp occur with increasing acid concentration - even when clay loading is moderate. Fig. 4.8: Contour plot illustrating the relationship between polarization, CNPs, and HCl Fig. 4.9: Surface plot illustrating the relationship between polarization, CNPs, and HCl These plots collectively demonstrate that optimal corrosion resistance is achieved through balanced clay nanoparticle content around 3.54.0 wt. combined with low-acid exposure conditions. Beyond these optimal points, performance gains diminish, reinforcing the importance of controlled formulation in coating design. OPTIMIZATION To determine the most effective balance between clay nanoparticle loading and acid concentration, an optimization study was carried out using Minitab-19 software. The goal was to maximize polarization resistance Rp, which directly reflects the coatings corrosion protection efficiency. Table 4.5 Polarization resistance design optimization targets. The table summarizes the design targets: CNPs wt. - allowed to vary between 1 and 5, with the aim of maximizing Rp. Acid concentration HCl - considered within the range 0.52.5 M, representing different levels of corrosive aggressiveness. The optimization output is illustrated in Figure 4.10, where a desirability value of 1.0 100 was achieved, signifying that the predicted conditions offer the best possible solution within the tested design space. Fig. 4.10: The optimization plot for maximum charge transfer resistance. According to the model: The maximum polarization resistance 546.76 Ω is achieved at approximately 3.48 wt. CNPs and 0.5 M HCl. These conditions provide a coating with excellent electrochemical stability, highlighting the protective role of clay nanoparticles at an optimal concentration. However, it was also observed that exceeding this clay loading may not further enhance performance, as excessive nanoparticles can agglomerate, creating weak points within the coating. Similarly, increasing acid concentration consistently reduced Rp, underlining the aggressive impact of acidic environments on coating durability. Overall, the optimization analysis demonstrates that careful adjustment of clay nanoparticle concentration - particularly around the 3.54.0 wt. range - under mild acidic exposure yields the most desirable balance between mechanical reinforcement and corrosion resistance. CORROSION RATE The corrosion resistance of the epoxyclay coatings was further evaluated by calculating the relative corrosion rate RCR of each sample. The RCR was determined as the reciprocal of the measured polarization resistance Rp, expressed as: RCR1Rp where Rp represents the charge transfer resistance obtained from the EIS measurements. Table 4.6 presents the corrosion rate values for all 20 samples. Table 4.6: Corrosion rate of the 20 coated samples The results reveal several clear trends: Samples with 0 clay 19 and 20 exhibited the highest corrosion rates RCR  0.00430.0048, confirming the poor barrier performance of unreinforced epoxy coatings. Introducing clay nanoparticles significantly reduced corrosion rates, with 5 wt. samples 1318 showing the lowest RCR values 0.001850.00189, indicating strong resistance to corrosive attack. Within each clay concentration, an increase in acid molarity led to higher corrosion rates. For instance, at 3 wt. clay, Rp decreased from 525 Ω in 0.5 M HCl Sample 7 to 499.8 Ω in 2.5 M HCl Sample 11. The overall trend confirms that both clay reinforcement and acid concentration play dominant but opposing roles - clay improves corrosion resistance, while higher acid concentration accelerates coating degradation. These findings validate the hypothesis that clay nanoparticle incorporation enhances the protective ability of epoxy coatings, with the greatest benefits observed at higher clay loadings under less aggressive acidic conditions. EXPERIMENTAL DATA FOR VALIDATION OF MINITAB OPTIMIZATION INTERPRETATION OF NYQUIST AND BODE PLOTS Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy EIS was conducted on the optimized sample OS identified through Minitab analysis 3.48 wt. CNPs, 0.5 M HCl. The results were represented using Nyquist and Bode plots Figure 4.11. In the Nyquist plot, the diameter of the semicircular arc corresponds to the polarization resistance Rp, which reflects the coatings corrosion protection efficiency. A larger arc diameter signifies greater charge transfer resistance and better barrier properties. In the Bode plot, both the impedance magnitude Z and phase angle are plotted against frequency. At low frequencies, the high Z values observed confirm strong resistance to ionic transport through the coating. The phase angle trending towards 45 in the mid-frequency range indicates good capacitive behavior of the system, consistent with effective barrier protection. Together, these plots confirm that the optimized formulation provides excellent corrosion resistance, aligning with the predicted outcomes from the optimization model. Fig. 4.11: Nyquist and Bode plots of the optimized sample OS. Table 4.7: Electrochemical impedance parameters for the optimized sample COMPARISON OF PREDICTED AND EXPERIMENTAL RESULTS The Minitab optimization predicted that the optimum conditions 3.48 wt. CNPs in 0.5 M HCl would yield a polarization resistance of  546.76 Ω. Experimental validation under these same conditions produced an Rp of 591.4 Ω, which is approximately 4.7 higher than the predicted value. This close agreement demonstrates the reliability of the statistical model and confirms that the clay-reinforced epoxy coating performs slightly better under real experimental conditions than predicted. The slight improvement may be attributed to synergistic nanoparticle dispersion and enhanced interfacial bonding within the epoxy matrix.

TEST RESULTS AND DISCUSSIONS ADHESION TEST RESULT AND DISCUSSIONS The adhesion properties of the coating systems were evaluated using the ASTM D3359 cross-cut tape test. Three samples were examined: Paint A  a conventional commercial coating Sikaflex. Paint B  another conventional commercial coating Hinaz. Optimal O  the optimized epoxy formulation reinforced with clay nanoparticles. The test procedure involved creating a grid of cuts through the coating film, applying adhesive tape, and then rapidly removing it. The percentage of coating removed was compared against ASTM D3359 adhesion rating standards. Results: Paint A Sikaflex: Assigned an adhesion rating of 1B 3565, showing extensive detachment and multiple squares removed. Paint B Hinaz: Similarly, rated 1B 3565, also demonstrating poor adhesion with significant peeling. Optimal O: Achieved an adhesion rating of 3B 515, with only minor detachment and minimal squares removed. Fig. 4.12: ASTM D3359 standard adhesion rating chart. Paint A Paint B Optimal O Fig. 4.13: Tape test images for Paint A, Paint B, and Optimal O. The comparative results clearly indicate that the clay-reinforced epoxy Optimal O significantly outperforms both conventional paints in terms of adhesion strength. The improved bonding performance is attributed to: Nanoparticle reinforcement, which provides a rougher microstructure, enhancing mechanical interlocking with the substrate. Reduced porosity, leading to stronger cohesive forces within the coating film. Improved epoxymetal interaction, supported by the presence of nanoparticles that enhance load transfer across the coatingsubstrate interface. In contrast, both Paint A and Paint B showed weak adhesion, making them more prone to early delamination under corrosive exposure. This highlights the practical benefits of incorporating clay nanoparticles into epoxy formulations, particularly for applications where long term durability and resistance to peeling are required. HARDNESS TEST RESULTS AND DISCUSSIONS The mechanical durability of the coatings was further assessed using the Rockwell hardness test, which evaluates a materials resistance to localized plastic deformation. Three coatings were examined: Optimal O  epoxy reinforced with clay nanoparticles. Paint A Sikaflex  conventional coating. Paint B Hinaz  conventional coating. Each coating was subjected to ten Rockwell hardness HRC measurements, along with corresponding indentation depths h, after which the total and average values were computed. OPTIMAL O Table 4.8: Hardness values for Optimal O PAINT A SIKAFLEX Table 4.9: Hardness values for Paint A Sikaflex PAINT B HINAZ Table 4.10: Hardness values for Paint B Hinaz Table 4.11: Comparison of hardness values across coatings The results reveal clear differences in mechanical performance among the coatings: Optimal O exhibited the highest average hardness 85.9 HRC, confirming that clay nanoparticle reinforcement significantly improves the epoxys resistance to indentation and wear. Paint A Sikaflex showed the lowest hardness 72.75 HRC, indicating weaker structural integrity and lower durability under mechanical stress. Paint B Hinaz displayed intermediate hardness 78.25 HRC, better than Paint A but still inferior to Optimal O. The enhanced hardness of Optimal O can be attributed to: Nanoparticle reinforcement, which strengthens the epoxy matrix by limiting polymer chain mobility. Improved load transfer, as the nanoparticles help distribute stress more evenly across the coating. Reduced micro-defects, since well-dispersed nanoparticles can fill voids and increase coating density. These findings confirm that nanoparticle incorporation not only enhances corrosion resistance but also provides superior mechanical durability, making clayepoxy coatings more suitable for long-term protective applications compared to conventional coatings. CHAPTER FIVE CONCLUSION AND RECOMMENDATIONS The present study investigated the effectiveness of clay nanoparticlereinforced epoxy coatings as a corrosion protection strategy for mild steel exposed to acidic environments. KEY FINDINGS Through detailed experimental, electrochemical, and mechanical analyses, several important conclusions can be drawn: Enhanced Surface Morphology: The SEM analysis revealed that the synthesized clay nanoparticles possessed irregular, porous structures that promote higher surface area and stronger interfacial adhesion between the coating and the substrate. This microstructural feature contributed significantly to the improved barrier properties of the nanoparticle- reinforced epoxy Ali et al., 2020; Mohammed et al., 2021. Superior Corrosion Resistance: The EIS results demonstrated that coatings containing 35 wt. clay nanoparticles consistently exhibited larger semicircle diameters in Nyquist plots, confirming enhanced charge transfer resistance Rp and lower corrosion rates. In contrast, the control coatings 0 wt. clay showed significantly weaker barrier performance, particularly at high acid concentrations. This confirms that nanoparticle reinforcement effectively impedes aggressive ion transport, thereby delaying corrosion processes Zhang et al., 2019; Gao et al., 2022. Statistical Validation and Optimization: Regression modeling and ANOVA analysis highlighted that clay nanoparticle content had a statistically significant positive influence on corrosion resistance, whereas increasing acid concentration produced a negative effect. The optimization study identified 3.48 wt. clay and 0.5 M HCl as the optimal formulation, producing an experimental Rp value of 591.4 Ω, slightly higher than predicted, thereby validating the accuracy and robustness of the optimization model Khan et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2023. Mechanical Improvements: The adhesion and hardness tests confirmed that the nanoparticle-reinforced epoxy coating exhibited superior adhesion 3B rating and highest hardness 85.9 HRC compared to conventional paints. These improvements are attributed to the ability of clay nanoparticles to reduce micro-voids, strengthen the epoxy matrix, and improve load distribution across the coating Sharma et al., 2018; Gupta et al., 2022. Sustainability Advantage: Beyond performance, clay nanoparticles present an eco-friendly and cost-effective alternative to conventional corrosion inhibitors such as chromates, which are environmentally hazardous. This aligns with current global trends in sustainable materials engineering, making clay-reinforced epoxy a viable solution for industries seeking greener alternatives Rahman et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021. In summary, the findings of this study confirm that epoxy reinforced with clay nanoparticles offers a synergistic improvement in structural, electrochemical, and mechanical performance, thereby providing a reliable, sustainable, and durable protective coating against corrosion in aggressive environments. RECOMMENDATIONS Based on the findings and limitations of this research, the following recommendations are made for further development and application: Industrial Application: Industries in oil and gas, marine, construction, and automotive sectors should consider adopting clayepoxy coatings as a sustainable alternative to conventional paints and coatings, especially in acidic or chloride- rich environments where corrosion risk is severe. Optimization of Nanoparticle Dispersion: Since nanoparticle agglomeration at higher loadings 5 wt. may reduce coating efficiency, further studies should focus on improving nanoparticle dispersion techniques, possibly through surfactants, ultrasonic mixing, or chemical functionalization of clay particles. Hybrid Reinforcement Studies: Future research should explore hybrid nanocomposites, combining clay nanoparticles with other eco- friendly reinforcements e.g., graphene oxide, silica, or bio-based inhibitors, to further enhance both corrosion resistance and mechanical strength. Field Testing and Long-Term Evaluation: While laboratory studies confirm strong performance, field trials under real-life service conditions e.g., pipelines, offshore structures, storage tanks are recommended to validate durability over extended exposure periods. Environmental and Economic Analysis: Further life-cycle and costbenefit assessments should be conducted to quantify the economic savings and environmental advantages of using clayepoxy coatings compared to conventional systems. Such studies will help encourage wider adoption in industry. Advanced Characterization: Incorporating advanced analytical techniques such as X-ray diffraction XRD, atomic force microscopy AFM, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy XPS in future work will provide deeper insights into nanoparticle distribution, interfacial bonding, and corrosion inhibition mechanisms at the nanoscale. FINAL REMARK This study contributes to closing the research gap in sustainable corrosion protection by demonstrating that clay nanoparticles can be effectively incorporated into epoxy coatings to deliver superior protection and durability while being cost-effective and environmentally friendly. With further refinement and industrial scaling, nanoparticle- reinforced coatings hold significant promise for next-gen corrosion protection technologies. REFERENCES Adewole, K. K., Daramola, O. ABSTRACT In Industrial settings, corrosion is a major problem due to its adverse effects on materials, causing degradation and reduction in performance and increasing cost of maintenance. The aim of this study is to explore the incorporation of clay nanoparticles in epoxy resin as a novel organic mixture for the anti-corrosion coating of mild steel. The purpose is to enhance the corrosion resistance of mild steel, which is widely used in various industries but is prone to corrosion in harsh environmental conditions. The methodology involved synthesizing a series of epoxy resin coatings with varying concentrations of clay nanoparticles. The coatings were applied to mild steel substrates and subjected to Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy EIS tests to evaluate their corrosion resistance. Scanning Electron Microscopy SEM and Energy-Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy EDS were used to analyze the surface morphology and elemental composition of the coatings. The results demonstrated that the inclusion of clay nanoparticles significantly improved the corrosion resistance of the epoxy resin coatings. The EIS results showed higher impedance values for the coatings with clay nanoparticles, indicating better barrier properties. SEM and EDS analysis confirmed a more uniform and dense coating structure with the presence of clay nanoparticles which contributed to the enhanced protective properties. In conclusion, the incorporation of clay nanoparticles into epoxy resin coatings provides an effective approach to enhance the anti-corrosion performance of mild steel. This study suggests that such composite coatings can offer superior protection in corrosive environments, potentially extending the lifespan of mild steel components and reducing maintenance costs. Hardness values for Paint B Hinaz 75 Table 4.11: Comparison of hardness values across coatings 75 CHAPTER ONE INTRODUCTION BACKGROUND OF STUDY Corrosion is a significant issue in many industrial sectors, including oil and gas, marine, construction, and automotive industries, where mild steel is widely used due to its mechanical strength and cost-effectiveness. However, its susceptibility to corrosion poses significant challenges, leading to material degradation, safety risks, and economic loss. Corrosion occurs when a material, particularly a metal, undergoes chemical or electrochemical reactions with its environment, resulting in the degradation of its properties, loss of structural integrity, and ultimately, material failure. This not only affects the efficiency and reliability of equipment but also imposes substantial economic burdens due to the high costs associated with maintenance, repair, and replacement. The global cost of corrosion is estimated to exceed trillions of dollars annually, with a significant portion of these expenses attributed to the corrosion of mild steel structures and components NACE, 2016. Mild steel is widely used in industries because it offers a balance between mechanical strength and cost-effectiveness. However, its major drawback is its vulnerability to corrosion when exposed to aggressive environments. In industrial settings, especially in oil and gas, chemical processing, and marine sectors, mild steel is exposed to moisture, oxygen, and corrosive chemicals, which significantly accelerate the corrosion process. Corrosion not only deteriorates the mechanical properties of the material but also poses safety hazards and environmental risks. Therefore, the development of effective anti- corrosion strategies to protect mild steel is a critical concern for many industries. Over the years, researchers have investigated various methods to protect mild steel from corrosion, including the application of organic coatings, metallic layers, and inhibitors. Jones, 2018. Among these approaches, polymer-based coatings, especially epoxy coatings have gained popularity due to their good adhesion, chemical resistance, and durability. However, epoxy coatings alone are not fully effective in aggressive environments, as they may still allow the permeation of moisture and corrosive ions Li et al., 2017. To address this, nanotechnology has emerged as a promising area of research, offering potential enhancements to the protective properties of epoxy coatings by introducing nanoparticles, particularly clay nanoparticles, as reinforcements Umoren et al., 2019. Studies by Merachtsaki et al. 2017 and Javidparvar et al. 2021 focused on the synthesis of epoxy-clay nanocomposites for corrosion protection. Ramya et al. explored the effect of varying clay concentrations on corrosion resistance and found that an optimal concentration of clay nanoparticles could reduce the rate of corrosion by more than 50 in steel. Similarly, Javidparvar et al 2021. investigated the effect of nano-clay particle size and distribution, finding that finer and well-dispersed clay particles within the epoxy matrix led to enhanced durability and corrosion resistance under aggressive environmental conditions. These studies suggest that both the dispersion quality and concentration of clay nanoparticles play crucial roles in determining the effectiveness of epoxy-clay nanocomposites for corrosion protection. In another approach, researchers like Shi et al. 2020 and Karim et al. 2022 examined the performance of multi- layered nanocomposite coatings to enhance corrosion resistance. Shi et al. proposed a multi-functional nanocomposite coating by combining clay with other nano-reinforcements, such as graphene oxide, to achieve synergistic effects. Karim et al. expanded on this concept by integrating nanoclays with other anti-corrosive agents, achieving further enhancements in corrosion resistance and coating longevity. These studies underscore the growing interest in combining nanomaterials to create hybrid epoxy coatings with tailored properties. Building on this body of research, the present study aims to further explore and optimize the reinforcement of epoxy coatings with clay nanoparticles for mild steel corrosion protection. By refining factors such as clay concentration, particle dispersion, and coating thickness, this project seeks to provide a high-performance, cost-effective corrosion protection solution that can be applied in diverse industrial sectors. STATEMENT OF THE PROBLEM Mild steel is highly susceptible to corrosion, especially when exposed to harsh environmental conditions such as high humidity, saline atmospheres, and industrial pollutants. This corrosion leads to degradation in structural integrity, resulting in safety hazards, increased maintenance costs, and shorter material lifespans. Traditional epoxy coatings are widely used to protect mild steel due to their adhesive and chemical-resistant properties. 6. However, these coatings alone are insufficient to prevent corrosion effectively, as they can allow the permeation of moisture and corrosive ions over time, compromising their protective capabilities Al-Turaif, 2019 . The challenge, therefore, is to develop a cost-effective, durable coating that enhances the corrosion resistance of mild steel beyond the limitations of conventional epoxy. Recent studies suggest that reinforcing epoxy with clay nanoparticles could significantly improve its barrier properties, potentially making it an effective solution for corrosion protection Panahi et al., 2024. However, the optimal formulation, concentration, and dispersion of clay nanoparticles within epoxy matrices remain unclear, as does the impact of these parameters on the coatings long-term performance in various environments. This study aims to address this gap by investigating the effectiveness of clay nanoparticle- reinforced epoxy coatings on mild steel and identifying the conditions that maximize corrosion resistance.

AIM AND OBJECTIVES The aim of this project is to develop and evaluate the effectiveness of clay nanoparticles embedded in epoxy resin as an advanced organic anti-corrosion coating for mild steel, with the goal of enhancing the coatings resistance to corrosion and improving the mechanical, thermal and chemical properties of the coating in harsh industrial environments. The objectives for carrying out this project include; To synthesize and embed clay nanoparticles as a reinforcement agent in epoxy resin To apply and evaluate the epoxy-clay nanocomposite coatings performance on mild steel substrates by conducting practical tests, such as electrochemical impedance spectroscopy EIS, scanning electron microscopy SEM and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy EDS To optimize the synthesis and embedding process by adjusting the ratio of clay nanoparticles to epoxy resin in order to achieve the best balance between corrosion resistance, mechanical strength and application efficiency, and thereby compare the performance with conventional epoxy coatings in terms of corrosion protection and durability. SIGNIFICANCE OF THE RESEARCH The significance of this study lies in its potential to offer an innovative solution to one of the most pressing challenges faced by industries that rely heavily on mild steel corrosion. Corrosion is a costly and often dangerous problem, especially in sectors such as oil and gas, marine, chemical processing, construction, and transportation. The failure of mild steel components due to corrosion can lead to safety risks, environmental damage, production downtime, and significant financial losses. This research aims to provide a more effective and durable method of protecting mild steel from corrosion through the development of epoxy-clay nanocomposite coatings, offering several key contributions such as enhanced corrosion protection, improved mechanical properties, thermal and chemical stability, cost-effective solution, environmental impact, advancement in nanotechnology applications, industrial relevance and scalability e.t.c. SCOPE OF THE RESEARCH The scope of this study encompasses several key areas related to the synthesis, formulation, application, and evaluation of clay nanoparticle-embedded epoxy coatings for corrosion protection of mild steel. The research focuses on the development of an advanced anti-corrosion coating that enhances the performance of mild steel in aggressive environments. The studys scope is defined by the following; Material Synthesis and Preparation Formulation of Epoxy-Clay Nanocomposite Coating Coating Application Characterization of the Nanocomposite Coating Corrosion Resistance Testing Mechanical Property Evaluation Comparative Analysis with Conventional Coatings Optimization of Formulation and Application Parameters Industrial Applicability and Recommendations CHAPTER TWO LITERATURE REVIEW BACKGROUND The basic principles of corrosion in mild steel stem from its interaction with moisture and oxygen, leading to the formation of rust, primarily composed of iron oxides. Jones 2018, describes corrosion as the degradation of materials properties due to interactions with their environment, encompassing chemical, electrochemical, and physical processes. This reaction is driven by the presence of an anodic and cathodic region on the steel surface, further influenced by environmental factors like humidity, temperature, and the presence of corrosive agents such as salts. Corrosion of metals, particularly mild steel, is a major issue in industrial applications due to its adverse effects on equipment lifespan, safety, and operational efficiency. Various techniques have been developed over the years to mitigate corrosion, among which protective coatings stand out as one of the most effective and widely used methods. In particular, organic coatings, such as epoxy resins, have been extensively utilized due to their ability to act as a barrier between the metal surface and corrosive environments. However, despite their widespread use, traditional epoxy coatings suffer from several limitations, such as poor resistance to mechanical damage, environmental degradation, and the eventual penetration of corrosive agents like water and oxygen. As a result, research efforts have focused on enhancing the properties of these coatings to improve their performance and durability, particularly in harsh industrial environments. CORROSION Corrosion, defined as the deterioration of materials, especially metals, due to chemical or electrochemical reactions with their environment, is a widespread problem affecting many industries. Corrosion is fundamentally an electrochemical process. Jones 2018 explains that in the presence of an electrolyte, metals undergo oxidation loss of electrons at the anode, while reduction reactions gain of electrons occur at the cathode. The relative positions of metals in the electrochemical series determine their corrosion tendencies; metals with a higher tendency to lose electrons, such as iron, are more prone to corrosion compared to noble metals like gold. Corrosion is not merely a technical problem but a human one, as it affects the safety, durability, and economics of infrastructure. Luo et. al 2022 highlights the human cost of corrosion, from the collapse of bridges to oil spills caused by corroded pipelines. Training engineers to detect early signs of corrosion and implement timely maintenance is just as crucial as technological solutions. Furthermore, fostering interdisciplinary collaboration between chemists, engineers, and environmental scientists has proven vital in developing comprehensive corrosion management strategies. As industries continue to rely on mild steel for critical infrastructure, the need for innovative approaches to corrosion prevention and management will only grow, driven by both technical advancements and human considerations. Studies like those by Bhattacharyya et al. 2022 illustrated how the local environment dictates the rate and nature of corrosion. In humid or marine environments, chloride ions significantly accelerate corrosion through the breakdown of passive oxide layers, exposing fresh metal to further oxidation. Industrial settings, with pollutants like sulfur dioxide and nitrogen oxides, also amplify corrosion rates, leading to complex iron sulfate and iron nitrate formations. SCIENCE OF CORROSION Corrosion occurs when a metal reacts to achieve a more thermodynamically stable state, usually forming oxides, hydroxides or sulfides. It typically involves the formation of an electrochemical cell, consisting of: Anode Oxidation Site: The site where the metal undergoes oxidation. For example, iron atoms lose electrons and enter the solution as ions. Fe  Fe2  2e- This reaction represents the metals dissolution, which weakens its structure Cathode Reduction Site: The site where a reduction reaction occurs, consuming the electrons released at the anode. For example, in the presence of oxygen and water, the common cathodic reaction is: O2  4e-  2H2O  4OH- Electrolyte: A conductive medium e.g., water with dissolved ions facilitates ion movement between the anode and cathode. Electrical Circuit: Electrons flow through the metal, connecting the anodic and cathodic sites, completing the electrochemical cell. The degradation product at the anode is determined by the specific metal and environmental conditions. For instance, Furcas et al. 2023 detailed how localized pH changes influence corrosion products like rust. The driving force for corrosion is the metals tendency to revert to its lower-energy state as an ore. Researchers like Zhang et al. 2020 further developed potential-pH diagrams to account for modern alloys and environmental variables, enhancing their predictive capabilities for corrosion in complex systems. Some metals, like aluminum and stainless steel, form a thin, adherent oxide layer that acts as a protective barrier, preventing further corrosion. However, factors such as chloride ions, studied by Shi et al. 2020 can destabilize this passive layer, leading to localized corrosion like pitting. Environmental variables such as oxygen, moisture and pollutants can propagate and accelerate corrosion. Microbiologically Influenced Corrosion MIC, studied by Jeffrey and Melchers 2015 illustrated the role of sulfate-reducing bacteria SRB in accelerating corrosion in marine environments, focusing on the mechanisms of microbial activity in steel degradation. MECHANISMS OF COROSSION IN MILD STEEL The corrosion of mild steel typically follows an electrochemical process where the metal acts as an anode and undergoes oxidation, releasing iron ions into the environment. Oxygen, water, or other corrosive agents act as electrolytes, facilitating the flow of electrons from the anode to the cathode, where reduction reactions occur. For instance, in the presence of water and oxygen, the released iron ions react to form hydrated iron oxide rust, which expands and weakens the metal structure. Various researchers explain that pitting, crevice, and galvanic corrosion are predominant forms of corrosion observed in various industrial applications such as Swagelok 2023 and NASA 2023. Understanding these different types of corrosion and their driving factors is essential for developing effective corrosion prevention and management strategies in industries where mild steel plays a critical role. These are the most common types of corrosion affecting mild steel; 2.2.2a PITTING CORROSION Pitting corrosion is a highly localized form of corrosion that results in the creation of small holes or pits in the material. These pits can penetrate deeply into the steel, causing significant damage despite the small surface area affected. Swagelok 2023 explains that pitting corrosion transpires when the protective chromium-rich oxide layer on stainless steel deteriorates, exposing the underlying metal to corrosive environments. This localized electrochemical attack leads to the formation of small cavities or pits. The presence of aggressive ions, such as chlorides, is a significant factor in this process. Once initiated, the pit acts as a localized anodic site, while the surrounding metal serves as the cathode, driving the corrosion process. Pitting is particularly dangerous because it can be difficult to detect until significant damage has occurred. Wilkinson Coutts Engineering Training Ltd 2023 emphasizes that pitting corrosion poses a serious threat to the integrity of metallic structures due to its highly localized nature. Even minimal overall mass loss can significantly weaken a structure, potentially resulting in sudden failures. Furthermore, the rate of pitting corrosion can vary significantly depending on the environment, making it challenging to predict and prevent.
